content.text: In FastAPI, the clean async SSE pattern for Claude is: use
inside an async generator, iterate over
. The Anthropic Python SDK explicitly supports this pattern and notes that
is a context manager yielding a stream with
is a lower-memory alternative that returns raw stream events.

attrs.alt: Async streaming responses with SSE in FastAPI concept diagram

content.text: SSE works well for Claude because the browser can receive incremental text over a single long-lived HTTP response, while your FastAPI backend stays fully async and simply relays the model’s output as it arrives. Claude’s Python SDK already abstracts the underlying stream and exposes a convenient iterator over text deltas, so your FastAPI code does not need to manually parse low-level event frames for the simplest case.
yields SSE frames to the client.

import os
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic

app = FastAPI()
client = AsyncAnthropic(api_key=os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"])

@app.post("/chat/stream")
async def chat_stream(request: Request, body: dict):
    prompt = body["message"]

    async def event_stream():
        async with client.messages.stream(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
            max_tokens=1024,
            messages=[
                {"role": "user", "content": prompt}
            ],
        ) as stream:
            async for text in stream.text_stream:
                if await request.is_disconnected():
                    await stream.close()
                    break
                yield f"data: {text}\n\n"

        yield "event: done\ndata: {}\n\n"

    return StreamingResponse(
        event_stream(),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={
            "Cache-Control": "no-cache",
            "X-Accel-Buffering": "no",
        },
    )
This follows the SDK’s documented async usage of
directly. The SDK also documents that the stream can be closed early with
, which is useful when the client disconnects or cancels the request.
Why this is the best default

lens is the best default when your frontend only needs progressive text output, because it gives you just the text deltas without requiring manual handling of every event type. That keeps your route small and focused on HTTP transport, while Anthropic’s SDK handles stream accumulation and event decoding.
This is especially clean in FastAPI because:
Cancellation and disconnect handling fit the async model well.
The frontend can consume it with
If you want lower memory overhead, the SDK documentation says you can use
, because it does not accumulate a final message object for you. This is useful when you only care about forwarding raw events and do not need helpers like

stream = await client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    stream=True,
)
Then you iterate through raw events rather than using
. The tradeoff is convenience versus memory efficiency:
The SDK also supports iterating over the stream itself to access structured events such as
. That matters when you want richer SSE semantics, like sending distinct event types to the browser instead of plain text chunks.
For example, Anthropic documents this event iteration pattern:
That means you can map SDK event types to SSE event names:

async def event_stream():
    async with client.messages.stream(...) as stream:
        async for event in stream:
            if event.type == "text":
                yield f"event: token\ndata: {event.text}\n\n"
            elif event.type == "message_stop":
                yield "event: done\ndata: {}\n\n"
This is a stronger pattern when you want the client to distinguish token output, tool JSON, and completion states.
If you later move beyond plain text streaming into tool-use flows, the SDK exposes

values for incremental JSON input construction. In FastAPI SSE terms, that means you can stream tool-call argument construction live to the frontend or to an internal observer UI, instead of waiting for the full tool input object.
So an advanced SSE relay could do this:
This is especially useful for debugging agent loops or building live developer consoles.
is that the SDK can accumulate a final message for you, and the docs describe helper methods such as
. These are useful if your FastAPI route needs to both stream live output and persist the final assistant message after the stream completes.

async def event_stream():
    async with client.messages.stream(...) as stream:
        async for text in stream.text_stream:
            yield f"data: {text}\n\n"

        final_text = await stream.get_final_text()
        # persist final_text to DB here

    yield "event: done\ndata: {}\n\n"
That gives you a clean “stream now, save later” architecture using the SDK’s documented helpers.
For FastAPI, keep these practical rules:
, not the sync client, inside async routes.
Put Claude streaming code in a service function, not directly in every route.
to stop work when the client leaves.
Return proper SSE headers so proxies do not buffer the stream.
Send a final completion event like
so the frontend can close cleanly.
These are standard FastAPI SSE practices, and the Anthropic SDK’s async stream interface fits them naturally because it is already built around async iteration and explicit close semantics.
A clean FastAPI module layout for SSE with Claude is:
— optional final-message saving after stream completion.
The route handles HTTP and headers.
The service handles Anthropic API calls.
Think of the whole system as two nested streams:
Claude streams tokens/events to your FastAPI backend.
FastAPI reformats those into SSE frames and streams them to the browser.
The simplest reliable implementation is therefore “Claude async stream in, SSE async stream out,” using
When working with async streaming responses with sse in fastapi, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with async streaming responses with sse in fastapi. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of async streaming responses with sse in fastapi scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
